finding: A dispositional trait (self-construal orientation) and situational factor (group size) jointly modulated choice behavior; the situational factor's effect was dependent on the dispositional trait level, mediated by differential attention allocation.

A trait measure of uncertainty in self-representation modulated likelihood of maintaining an option, but only when the option was relevant to identity; the effect was absent for non-identity-relevant options.

A perceptual manipulation (entity vs. attribute framing) shifted the strategy used during comparative evaluation, resulting in different preference ordering; the effect was mediated by perceived integration and moderated by motivation.
A linguistic property (quantificationality) of an upstream element facilitated processing of a downstream structure; the facilitation was selective to quantified but not definite reference.

When answering factual questions tapping politically salient content, respondents engaged in strategic misreporting rather than reflecting genuine misbelief; the behavior was stronger among those with higher engagement.
A manipulation of expectation altered the reference point used for evaluating an option, increasing valuation; the effect persisted across exchange and valuation contexts.
Position in a distribution modulated preference for risky redistribution and decisions favoring particular others; individuals near the bottom of the distribution showed distinct choice patterns.
A measure of the divergence between model-implied and realized variation predicted subsequent aggregate outcomes; high values of the metric forecasted high future levels of the outcome.

Five dimensions of a trait taxonomy each showed distinct associations with anatomical variation in the same anatomical system; different trait dimensions were associated with opposing patterns of the same anatomical features.

An early triggering event reduced a subsequent distal outcome; the effect operated through intermediate outcomes, and recovery from the initial effect was possible but dependent on timing in the developmental sequence.

A dispositional trait (age) predicted preference for a particular activity type under unspecified situational constraints; experimental manipulation of the situational constraint eliminated the trait effect.

An intensive treatment intervention increased performance on some standardized outcome measures relative to a comparison condition, but not others; treatment also increased the likelihood of downstream placement in a particular context.
A weak preceding stimulus reduced the perceived intensity of a subsequent intense stimulus; individuals with lower baseline sensitivity showed larger magnitude reductions.

A measure of online social behavior reliably predicted gray matter density in focal brain regions; the brain regions associated with online behavior were distinct from those associated with offline social behavior.
Individuals showed a preference for joint action over solitary action even when joint action was costlier; the preference was stable across contexts and individuals.

Formatting variations in code presentation showed no reliable effect on comprehension measures; perceived readability varied across conditions but did not correlate with actual comprehension performance.
Trait measures of affective disposition predicted relationship satisfaction in both samples; different specific traits predicted satisfaction in samples differing in relationship stage.
A latent factor structure extracted from multiple measures of a construct domain yielded multiple robust dimensions; these dimensions were conceptually distinct from a related established taxonomy.

The shape of decision weight functions differed across lotteries containing outcomes varying in affective salience; affect-rich outcomes produced more extreme weighting patterns than affect-poor outcomes.
Implicit learning of associations between neutral stimuli and valenced stimuli shifted evaluation of the neutral stimulus, even in the absence of explicit memory for the pairings.
